text: Oh dear, it's not a very good time to be Emma Watson, is it? From child actor to indie darling to big budget star, and finally to whiny, controversial, and divisive activist, increasingly disconnected from reality, and now publicly ridiculed by one of the biggest names in modern culture in what may go down as the most savage dressing down in history. Ever since she exploded onto the big screen with the Harry Potter franchise, her acting has carried her to the very cusp of A-level stardom before

it all came to a shuddering halt in one of the most baffling acts of career self-sabotage outside of Rachel Zaggler. >> It's Hollywood, baby. A woman who pinned her reputation, her public image, and her entire worldview on whatever happened to be most popular at the time, never considering that the cultural winds might change and what's popular today might not be popular tomorrow. So, saddle up, dear viewer, grab your wizarding wands and whatever other stupid [ __ ] they use in Harry Potter,

and let us examine the crash and burn of Emma Watson. Emma Charlotte Dair Watson. was born in Paris, France in 1990 to a pair of wealthy lawyers. So naturally, she grew up with all the benefits of having wealthy parents, private schooling, tuition, multiple homes, and access to any clubs and interests she wanted to pursue, one of which turned out to be acting. [ __ ] me, she basically is Hermione Granger. Anyway, by 1999, a series of books about a boy wizard were

turning out to be pretty popular, and work soon began on adapting them into a movie franchise. For this, they needed a bunch of child actors to play the three leads, including Hermione. Their searching soon led them to the Oxford Acting School, where Watson was studying, and after no fewer than eight auditions, she was finally cast in the role. And well, all I can say about her early performances is you can tell that she was a theater kid. That's for [ __ ] sure.

>> I'm Hermione Granger. And you are? >> Piss taken aside though, for a 10-year-old that had never acted in a movie before, I guess she was fine. Daniel Radcliffe was way better, though. Anyway, you don't need me to tell you that the Harry Potter movies would go on to become a global phenomenon over the next several years, keeping Watson and her co-stars so busy that she literally didn't act in anything else until 2007 when she appeared in the TV movie Ballet

Shoes, a film that landed mixed reviews and didn't do a whole lot for her career. In public, the Harry Potter series was going from strength to strength. But behind the scenes, the cracks were starting to show. Watson and the others were burned out by the constant demands of filming and promotional tours. And when it came time to renegotiate her contract after The Goblet of Fire, she seriously considered leaving the franchise altogether. Still, she was eventually convinced to sign on

again. It's amazing what a multi-million dollar paycheck can do for a girl, isn't it? And by 2011, the series came to a close with Deathly Hallows Part Two. For the first time in more than a decade, Watson was on her own. She'd basically grown up with Harry Potter. She'd never had a normal childhood or lived a normal life and she likely never would. Keep that one in mind because it'll be important later. The question now was how to escape the long shadow of Homari

Granger and forge a career of her own. Her first few outings were mostly low-budget indie movies. Everything from biopics like My Week with Marilyn to romantic dramas like The Perks of Being a Wallflower and even heist movies like Bling Ring. None of them were big hits, but they did at least prove that she could be more than a mildly annoying bookworm with crappy hair. 2014 saw a move into big budget film making with Darren Aronowski's Noah, followed up by low-budget thrillers, Colonia and

Regression. No matter the budget or the quality of the movie, one thing that stayed consistent with the positive reviews for Watson's acting. Now in her mid20s and arguably at the peak of her career, she was developing a reputation as an eclectic and confident performer, willing to take on challenging roles and play against type, equally at home on the big or the small screen. An actor who wasn't just chasing fame and money, but taking on projects with actual artistic merit. Well done, Emma. Her

star was rising. She was rapidly becoming the new ITG girl of Hollywoods. Young talented intelligent and sophisticated. She could navigate interviews like a pro. She was a natural in front of the camera and she held all the correct opinions on important political topics. When the Me Too movement kicked off, Emma was all over it like a fly on [ __ ] making speeches about feminism, establishing activism groups, and even getting appointed to the UN as their goodwill ambassador for

women. Because truly, no one understands the plight of women in repressive third world regimes better than a multi-millionaire, privately educated theater kid, Hollywood actress that's never held a normal job in her entire life. really. >> But more on that later. Pretty soon, Watson wasn't content just to make speeches in front of rooms full of elderly politicians who probably wish they were literally anywhere else. She was bringing her activism directly into the movies she starred in. The

liveaction Beauty and the Beast was rewritten at her request to make Belle into an inventor and engineer because >> girls get it done. >> Her political worldview could best be described as I support current thing. also known as the Mark Ruffalo technique. No matter the issue, no matter the complexity or the nuance, you could pretty much guarantee that Emma Watson would be right there in the thick of it, pushing whatever the progressive consensus happened to be at the time.

She was less of a person with her own opinions and ideas and more like a series of safe, politically correct talking points wrapped up in a flesh suit, all delivered with the kind of sneering upper class British disdain that can only truly come from playing life on easy mode from the day she was born. But just like an overloaded cauldron filled with the wrong ingredients, problems were a brewing. Dear viewer, remember the lady who wrote all those wizarding books? Well, it

turns out she'd become quite rich and famous over the years. And as a big raging progressive feminist herself, she and Emma were marching in lock step on almost every issue, all except one. It turns out that the wizarding lady had a few issues with the tea part of LGBT and began voicing her concerns with increasing volume. And the reaction from the other side was, "Well, how can I put this

now?" Now, Emma was in a bit of a tricky spot because she'd made it her mission in life to support Current Thing. But now, the wizarding lady who she owed her entire career in stardom to was openly attacking one of the most sacred parts of Current Thing. What to do? Should she stand firm beside her friend of 20 years and back her up? Should she maintain a dignified silence and refuse to get drawn into a fight that wasn't hers? Or should she turn her back on the

wizarding lady, hang her out to dry, and make increasingly passive aggressive attacks against her? I think you can guess which one she chose. And the thing is that all work great when Emma seemed to be on the side of current thing and the wizarding lady definitely wasn't. The problem with current thing though is that eventually it tends to change and by 2025 the cultural winds had shifted dramatically. Public opinion on the issue was changing and suddenly the wizarding lady wasn't

seen as the evil witch who had to be defeated anymore. Now people were beginning to actually listen and even openly support her. And it began to occur to Emma that she might have backed the wrong horse on this one. [ __ ] So, what to do? Stand her ground and maintain her position even if it wasn't so popular anymore? Or do a complete about face and pretend like she'd never disagreed with the wizarding lady in the first place? Well, the answer came on September the 24th on J.

Shett's on purpose podcast where Emma claimed that she loved the wizarding lady and supported her and would always be open to talking with her. Oh, really? That's interesting. Coming from someone who spent half a decade publicly [ __ ] on her. Now, if you were feeling forgiving, you could probably write this whole thing off as a slightly cowardly but very pragmatic decision by someone who senses the political sands moving beneath her and doesn't want to get pulled down by them.

And that might have been all she wrote, except this particular interview came to the attention of the wizarding lady, who until now had stayed pretty quiet on the topic of young Emma. Not anymore, as it turned out, when she decided to deliver the mother of all dress and downs a couple of days later in a very lengthy Twitter post, calling out Watson as a spineless grifter, a shameless opportunist, a fair weather friend, and a spoiled, privileged brat that had

never worked a day in her life, and was only trying to cozy up to her again because it was politically safe. Now, >> damn, bro. I've seen Mortal Kombat fatalities that were more forgiven than this. I've seen many a debate, many a scathing put down and brutal rebuttal in my time, but never in all of my days have I witnessed a person publicly dismantled with such brutal finesse. It wasn't just a takedown. It was a full-scale verbal evisceration and proof that if there's one group of people you

really don't want to piss off in the online space, it's [ __ ] writers. especially billionaire writers with hundreds of millions of fans behind them who built their entire careers out of basically nothing. With that one post, Emma Watson's public persona was transformed from a slightly preachy but relatively respected activist to the living personification of everything that people find so repellent about Hollywood actors. The smug patronizing I know better than you attitude. The

echochamber mentality that comes from a life of privilege and luxury. the mindlessly predictable conformity masquerading as sophisticated intellectualism. And worst of all, the willingness to abandon any position the moment it becomes unpopular. She wasn't just opinionated and insufferable. She was a coward. And for someone who built their entire brand on fearlessly speaking truth to power, well, that was the most damaging revelation of all. So, what's next for Emma Watson? Can she

possibly claw her way back from this? Well, her movie career basically ended 5 years ago when she pulled the plug on acting to focus on activism. So, I don't really know if there's any momentum left behind her, even if she wanted to make a comeback. She's 35 now, which isn't exactly old, but it ain't exactly young either, and it's definitely not a good age to try to build your career back up after wasting your prime years in the wilderness. She's not likely to make

much progress as an activist either now that her cover's been blown, and she's willing to soften her position on the whole tea issue. Because if there's one thing that particular group of activists won't accept, it's betrayal. So, it kind of feels like she's [ __ ] Either way, Emma Watson really is proof that if you stand for nothing, well, you'll fall for anything. Anyway, that's all I've got for today. Go away now.
